macro_events.event_summary: Airlines are cutting flight capacity as jet fuel costs surge due to the Iran war, pressuring airline margins while boosting energy names.
primary_drivers: Jet fuel/crude price spike from Middle East conflict raising airline operating costs
Capacity cuts reducing airline revenue and travel-adjacent demand
Higher crude prices directly benefiting upstream oil producers and refiners with crack spread expansion

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream exposure; Brent-linked realizations rise directly with war-driven crude spike, and refining margins widen as distillate/jet cracks blow out.
Same upstream leverage as XOM; earnings estimates revise higher as crude stays elevated on Iran risk premium.
Pure-play refiner — jet fuel/distillate crack spreads expand sharply when jet prices surge faster than crude input, directly boosting refining margins.
Large US refiner with heavy distillate/jet exposure; benefits from the exact crack spread widening that is squeezing airlines.
High-beta US E&P with Permian leverage; cash flow highly sensitive to sustained crude above risk-premium levels.
Reduced flight capacity and higher travel costs marginally shift enterprise travel toward videoconferencing; peer-rotation narrative trade rather than fundamental shift.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs; United has the most international/long-haul exposure among US majors, making it most vulnerable to both fuel cost and Middle East route disruption. Capacity cuts directly reduce revenue.
Large fuel cost exposure despite refinery hedge (Monroe); international premium traffic exposed to war disruption; analyst EPS cuts likely as capacity trims flow through.
Highest leverage among majors — balance sheet least able to absorb sustained fuel shock; unhedged fuel policy means full cost passthrough.
Domestic low-cost carrier with thin margins; historically hedged but hedges rolling off, and price-sensitive leisure demand hit hardest by fare increases needed to offset fuel.
Weakest balance sheet among listed airlines; fuel shock on top of already negative FCF raises going-concern narrative.
Global online travel agency — flight capacity cuts and higher fares directly reduce booking volumes and take-rate dollars; international exposure amplifies impact.
Same mechanism as BKNG — reduced flight inventory and weaker leisure demand from higher fares compress bookings.
Capacity cuts reduce near-term aircraft delivery urgency; airlines under fuel stress defer orders and slow fleet growth, pressuring order book narrative.

headlines: Airlines cut flights as fuel costs surge — an economic fallout from the Iran war that markets may be missing
